cover.body_html:
41页。

7月10日，苹果公司向北加州联邦地区法院递交了一份长达41页的诉状，把OpenAI、OpenAI的硬件子公司io Products，以及两名前苹果员工Chang Liu和Tang Yew Tan一并告上法庭。案件编号5:26-cv-07078。

这不是什么小打小闹的员工跳槽纠纷。诉状开篇第一句就没打算客气："本案关乎苹果前员工窃取苹果商业机密，系统性地帮助OpenAI加速其消费级硬件业务。"翻译一下就是：OpenAI你偷了我东西，而且是有组织、有计划、成建制地偷。

更微妙的是被告名单里的那个名字——Jony Ive。这位苹果前首席设计官，在2019年离开苹果后创办了设计公司LoveFrom，而io Products正是OpenAI与LoveFrom的合资企业。换句话说，苹果曾经的设计灵魂人物，现在成了被告方的核心成员。

这起诉讼的分量，怎么高估都不为过。一边是全球市值最高的公司，掌控着消费电子行业最严密的供应链和最成熟的硬件方法论；另一边是当前最炙手可热的AI公司，正在从软件向硬件渗透。两边在法庭上的第一次正面碰撞，预示着AI产业的竞争维度，正在从"谁的模型更强"升级到"谁的硬件生态更牢"。

诉状里的"实锤"：从聊天记录到原型零件

苹果的诉状写得相当不客气，甚至可以说"杀气腾腾"。里面披露的细节，每一个单独拿出来都够写一篇头条。

第一个细节是人才掠夺的规模。苹果指控，OpenAI系统性地挖走了苹果的硬件工程师，总数超过400人。这些人分布在芯片设计、传感器、显示技术、产品设计等多个核心部门。400人是什么概念？相当于把一个中型硬件创业公司的核心团队整建制搬过去了。

如果只是挖人，那还属于硅谷的"正常操作"——毕竟员工有择业自由。但苹果的指控远不止于此。

第二个细节，也是最有冲击力的：带苹果零件去面试。诉状称，被告之一的Chang Liu在面试OpenAI职位时，随身携带了苹果未发布产品的原型零件和内部工程样品，用来展示自己的技术能力。你没看错——不是PPT，不是设计图，是实体零件。

这个细节的杀伤力在于：它把"商业机密盗窃"从抽象的知识产权概念，变成了一个非常具体、非常有画面感的行为。你面试的时候把前东家还没发布的产品零件揣兜里，说"你看我做过这个"——这在任何一家科技公司都是红线中的红线。

第三个细节是聊天记录里的"LOL"。诉状引用了被告员工的内部聊天记录，其中一人在讨论挖苹果员工的策略时，用了"LOL"（大笑）的表情。苹果的律师显然很擅长讲故事——这个"LOL"被放在诉状非常靠前的位置，意在塑造一种"OpenAI对窃取苹果机密毫不在意、甚至以此为乐"的形象。

第四个细节是io Products的成立时间线。苹果指出，Tang Yew Tan在苹果任职期间，就已经开始秘密参与io Products的创建工作，违反了竞业协议和保密义务。也就是说，人还在苹果上班，心思已经在帮OpenAI做硬件了。

这些细节叠加在一起，构成了苹果叙事的完整链条：OpenAI有组织地挖苹果硬件人才→核心员工携带机密信息和原型零件→在苹果在职期间就秘密筹备竞品公司→系统性地加速OpenAI的硬件业务。

"400多名前苹果员工、实体原型零件、在职期间秘密筹备竞品——这已经不是普通的人才流动，这是成建制的技术转移。"—— Dawn Vision编辑部

为什么是现在？OpenAI的硬件野心与苹果的焦虑

诉讼选在7月10日这个时间点，非常值得玩味。

就在几天前的7月10日，OpenAI刚刚发布了GPT-5.6系列模型，同步推出了ChatGPT Work企业级智能体产品。这是OpenAI从"对话AI"向"工作AI"战略转型的标志性产品。而就在同一天，苹果把诉状递到了法院。

是巧合吗？可能是。但更可能的是，苹果选在OpenAI风头正劲的时候出手，本身就是一种策略——在你最得意的时候给你一记重拳，让市场和用户都看到：你光鲜的产品背后，可能有不干净的东西。

更深层的背景是：OpenAI正在认真做硬件，而这正在触及苹果的核心利益。

OpenAI的硬件野心已经不是秘密。从2024年开始，关于OpenAI硬件项目的传闻就没断过：有说是AI眼镜，有说是家庭智能设备，有说是类似Alexa的AI助手硬件。Jony Ive的加入更是坐实了这件事——如果只是做个普通的智能音箱，犯得着请苹果的灵魂设计师出山吗？

更关键的是，OpenAI的硬件路线和苹果的战略方向高度重合。苹果这些年在AI硬件上押了重注：Apple Watch的健康AI、AirPods的AI音频、Vision Pro的空间计算、iPhone端侧AI——每一条都是苹果的核心赛道。如果OpenAI带着大模型的优势杀入硬件市场，加上Jony Ive的设计能力，那对苹果来说威胁就不是"又一个竞争对手"那么简单了。

苹果的焦虑还来自另一个维度：人才的持续流失。400多名前苹果员工去了OpenAI，这个数字本身就很说明问题。为什么苹果的硬件工程师愿意去OpenAI？因为在那里，他们做的是"AI时代的下一代硬件"，而在苹果，他们可能还在迭代第N代iPhone的细节。对于顶尖工程师来说，"定义一个新品类"的吸引力，远远大于"优化一个成熟产品"。

这才是苹果真正担心的：如果OpenAI成功定义了AI时代的消费硬件品类，而苹果因为自己的AI进展缓慢而掉队，那损失的就不只是市场份额，而是整个生态的主导权。就像当年智能手机颠覆功能机时代一样——不是你做得不够好，而是游戏规则变了。

AI硬件的下一个战场：从"谁模型强"到"谁体验好"

把这起诉讼放到更大的产业背景下看，你会发现一个清晰的趋势：AI产业的竞争重心，正在从软件向硬件迁移。

过去三年，所有人都在比"谁的模型参数更大""谁的benchmark分数更高""谁的推理更便宜"。但到了2026年，大模型能力本身的差异化正在缩小——GPT-5.6、Claude 3.5、Gemini 2.0、DeepSeek V3，这些头部模型在大多数任务上的表现已经拉不开代差。

当模型能力不再是瓶颈，体验就成了新的竞争维度。而体验的载体，就是硬件。

为什么？因为AI最终要服务于人的真实需求，而人的需求是在具体场景里发生的——你在厨房做饭时需要的AI助手，和你在办公桌前写代码时需要的AI助手，和你开车时需要的AI助手，形态完全不同。软件是通用的，但硬件是场景化的。谁能把AI能力装进最合适的硬件形态里，谁就能占领用户的真实生活场景。

这就是为什么OpenAI要做硬件、为什么Google在押注Pixel和Gemini设备、为什么Meta在做AR眼镜、为什么苹果在Vision Pro上砸了这么多钱——大家都在赌：AI时代的计算平台是什么形态？是眼镜？是耳机？是家庭机器人？还是某种我们现在还想象不出来的东西？

在这场赌局里，苹果有自己的优势：几十年的硬件设计经验、成熟的供应链、庞大的用户基数、强大的隐私品牌。但苹果也有自己的软肋：AI模型能力相对落后、组织文化偏保守、在新形态硬件上（比如Vision Pro）还没有证明自己。

而OpenAI的优势在于：最领先的大模型能力、最前沿的AI产品理解、足够高的品牌热度、以及Jony Ive这样的设计教父加持。但OpenAI的劣势也很明显：零硬件经验、没有供应链积累、没有线下渠道、没有用户信任基础（尤其是在隐私和安全方面）。

所以，OpenAI从苹果挖400多个硬件工程师，逻辑就非常清楚了——我缺硬件能力，而你苹果有最好的硬件人才，那我就直接把人挖过来。这是最快、最直接的补课方式。至于这种方式合不合法、会不会吃官司，从OpenAI的角度看，可能是"先做了再说，大不了赔钱"——只要能抢在别人之前把AI硬件做出来，那点诉讼费根本不算什么。

终局判断：AI竞争进入"全栈对抗"时代

苹果诉OpenAI这起案件，最终的判决结果可能要等好几年。但它的真正意义，不在于谁输谁赢，而在于它标志着一个时代的开始。

第一个信号：AI公司的竞争，已经从"模型层"进入"全栈层"。

两年前，AI公司的竞争还是比谁的模型更强、谁的API更便宜。现在，竞争维度已经全面扩展：模型能力、产品体验、硬件形态、开发者生态、企业服务、安全合规——每一个环节都是战场。OpenAI做硬件、做企业服务、做操作系统；苹果做大模型、做端侧AI、做空间计算——大家都在往对方的地盘里渗透。

未来的AI巨头，不会是只做模型的公司，也不会是只做硬件的公司，而是"模型+芯片+硬件+软件+服务"全栈打通的公司。在这个维度上，苹果和OpenAI的碰撞，只是一个开始。

第二个信号：人才流动的法律边界，会在AI时代被重新定义。

硅谷的传统是"人才自由流动"——工程师从A公司跳到B公司，带着经验和方法论，只要不带走具体的代码和文档，一般都没事。但在AI时代，这个边界变得模糊了。一个参与过苹果芯片设计的工程师，他脑子里的知识和经验，算不算"商业机密"？他能不能把这些经验用在新公司的芯片项目上？

更复杂的是AI模型本身。如果一个工程师在苹果用苹果的数据训练了某个模型，然后他去了OpenAI，把类似的方法用在OpenAI的模型上——这算不算侵权？这些问题，目前的法律体系还没有明确答案。苹果诉OpenAI这起案件，很可能会成为一个标杆性的判例，重新划定AI时代人才流动和知识迁移的法律红线。

第三个信号：消费电子的下一个十年，由AI定义。

过去十五年，消费电子行业的主旋律是智能手机。iPhone定义了整个移动互联网时代的硬件形态和交互方式。但智能手机的创新已经明显放缓——每年的新iPhone都是"更亮一点、更快一点、摄像头好一点"，没有本质变化。

AI正在成为消费电子新的驱动力。端侧大模型、AI眼镜、空间计算、家庭机器人、AI可穿戴设备——这些新赛道里，很可能会诞生下一个"iPhone时刻"。苹果和OpenAI在法庭上的碰撞，本质上是两家公司在争夺"AI时代消费电子定义者"的位置。

当然，这起诉讼也充满了未知数。苹果能拿出多少实锤证据？OpenAI会如何抗辩？Jony Ive在其中扮演了什么角色？最终的赔偿金额会是多少？这些问题，可能要等几年后才有答案。

但有一件事是确定的：从这份41页诉状被递交到法院的那一刻起，AI产业的竞争就进入了一个新阶段。不再是创业者之间的小打小闹，而是科技巨头之间的全栈对抗。法庭上的唇枪舌剑，只是这场战争的序幕。

真正的较量，在产品里。在用户手里。在每一台即将到来的AI硬件里。

明天见。
cover.body_html_en:
41 pages.

On July 10, Apple filed a 41-page complaint in the Northern District of California, suing OpenAI, its hardware subsidiary io Products, and two former Apple employees — Chang Liu and Tang Yew Tan. Case number 5:26-cv-07078.

This isn't some petty employee poaching dispute. The very first line of the complaint pulls no punches: "This case is about Apple's former employees stealing Apple's trade secrets to systematically help OpenAI accelerate its consumer hardware business." Translation: OpenAI, you stole from us, and you did it organizationally, deliberately, and at scale.

The most intriguing name on the defendant list? Jony Ive. Apple's former chief design officer, who left the company in 2019 to found design firm LoveFrom, is connected to io Products — the joint hardware venture between OpenAI and LoveFrom. The man who was once Apple's design soul is now a core figure on the defendant's side.

It's hard to overstate the weight of this lawsuit. On one side, the world's most valuable company, controlling the consumer electronics industry's tightest supply chain and most mature hardware methodology. On the other, the hottest AI company on the planet, now pushing from software into hardware. Their first direct courtroom collision signals that AI industry competition is escalating from "who has the better model" to "who has the stronger hardware ecosystem."

The "Receipts" in the Complaint: From Chat Logs to Prototype Parts

Apple's complaint is written with barely concealed aggression. The details disclosed within — each one alone is headline-worthy.

First detail: the scale of talent poaching. Apple alleges that OpenAI has systematically hired away Apple's hardware engineers, totaling more than 400 people. These individuals span core divisions: chip design, sensors, display technology, product design. 400 people — that's essentially the entire core team of a mid-sized hardware startup, moved over lock, stock, and barrel.

If it were just hiring, that'd be "normal" Silicon Valley behavior — employees have the right to change jobs. But Apple's allegations go much further.

Second detail, and the most explosive: bringing Apple parts to job interviews. The complaint claims that defendant Chang Liu, when interviewing for a position at OpenAI, carried prototype parts and internal engineering samples from unreleased Apple products to demonstrate his technical capabilities. Read that again: not PowerPoint slides, not design documents — physical parts.

What makes this detail so damaging is that it transforms "trade secret theft" from an abstract intellectual property concept into something very specific, very vivid, and very damning. Showing up to a job interview with your former employer's unreleased product parts in your pocket, saying "look what I worked on" — that's a red line at any tech company. The reddest of red lines.

Third detail: the "LOL" in chat logs. The complaint quotes internal chat messages among defendant employees, where one person used a "LOL" emoji while discussing strategies for poaching Apple employees. Apple's lawyers clearly know how to tell a story — that "LOL" is placed very early in the complaint, designed to paint a picture of "OpenAI doesn't care about stealing Apple's secrets; they find it funny."

Fourth detail: the io Products timeline. Apple alleges that Tang Yew Tan, while still employed at Apple, was already secretly working on the creation of io Products, violating non-compete and confidentiality obligations. Translation: still on Apple's payroll, already helping OpenAI build competing hardware.

These details stacked together form the complete arc of Apple's narrative: OpenAI systematically poaches Apple hardware talent → key employees bring confidential information and prototype parts → they secretly prepare competing products while still on Apple's payroll → all of this systematically accelerates OpenAI's hardware business.

"400+ ex-Apple employees, physical prototype parts, secretly building a competitor while still on the payroll — this isn't normal talent flow. This is technology transfer at scale."— The Dawn Vision Editorial Desk

Why Now? OpenAI's Hardware Ambitions and Apple's Anxiety

The timing of the lawsuit — July 10 — is very much worth examining.

Just days earlier, on July 10, OpenAI had just released its GPT-5.6 model family and launched ChatGPT Work, its enterprise-level agent product. It was a landmark moment in OpenAI's strategic pivot from "conversational AI" to "work AI." And on that very same day, Apple filed its complaint.

Coincidence? Maybe. But it's more likely that Apple chose to strike while OpenAI was riding high — a deliberate strategy to land a punch at your opponent's moment of maximum glory, so that the market and users both see: behind your shiny product, there might be something dirty.

The deeper context: OpenAI is serious about hardware, and it's touching Apple's core interests.

OpenAI's hardware ambitions are no secret. Since 2024, rumors about OpenAI hardware projects have never stopped — AI glasses, home smart devices, an Alexa-like AI assistant hardware. Jony Ive's involvement confirms it: if this were just another smart speaker, you don't bring in Apple's design godfather for that.

More critically, OpenAI's hardware roadmap and Apple's strategic direction overlap heavily. Apple has bet big on AI hardware in recent years: health AI on Apple Watch, AI audio on AirPods, spatial computing with Vision Pro, on-device AI on iPhone — each one a core Apple battleground. If OpenAI enters the hardware market with its LLM advantages plus Jony Ive's design capabilities, the threat to Apple isn't just "another competitor." It's existential.

Apple's anxiety comes from another dimension too: continuing talent erosion. 400+ former Apple employees going to OpenAI — that number speaks for itself. Why are Apple's hardware engineers willing to go to OpenAI? Because there, they're building "the next generation of AI-era hardware," whereas at Apple, they might be iterating on the Nth generation of iPhone details. For top engineers, the appeal of "defining a new category" far outweighs "optimizing a mature product."

That's what Apple truly fears: if OpenAI successfully defines the consumer hardware category of the AI era, and Apple falls behind due to its own slow AI progress, the loss isn't just market share — it's dominion over the entire ecosystem. Just like how smartphones flipped the feature phone era — it's not that you weren't good enough; it's that the rules of the game changed.

The Next AI Hardware Battleground: From "Better Model" to "Better Experience"

Zoom out and place this lawsuit in the broader industry context, and a clear trend emerges: the competitive center of gravity in AI is shifting from software to hardware.

For the past three years, everyone was comparing "who has more parameters," "who scores higher on benchmarks," "who has cheaper inference." But by 2026, the differentiation in raw model capability among top players is narrowing — GPT-5.6, Claude 3.5, Gemini 2.0, DeepSeek V3 — these leading models are no longer generations apart on most tasks.

When model capability is no longer the bottleneck, experience becomes the new competitive dimension. And the carrier of experience is hardware.

Why? Because AI ultimately serves real human needs, and human needs happen in specific contexts — the AI assistant you need while cooking in the kitchen is completely different from the one you need while coding at your desk, which is completely different from the one you need while driving. Software is universal; hardware is contextual. Whoever can package AI capabilities into the right hardware form factor will occupy users' real-life scenarios.

That's why OpenAI is making hardware, why Google is betting on Pixel and Gemini devices, why Meta is building AR glasses, why Apple has poured so much into Vision Pro — everyone is gambling: what will the computing platform of the AI era look like? Glasses? Earbuds? Home robots? Or something we can't even imagine yet?

In this gamble, Apple has its advantages: decades of hardware design experience, a mature supply chain, a massive user base, a strong privacy brand. But Apple also has weaknesses: relatively落后的 AI model capabilities, a conservative organizational culture, and unproven new form factors (looking at you, Vision Pro).

OpenAI's advantages: leading LLM capabilities, cutting-edge AI product understanding, enormous brand heat, and a design godfather like Jony Ive. But OpenAI's disadvantages are equally obvious: zero hardware experience, no supply chain accumulation, no offline channels, no foundation of user trust (especially around privacy and security).

So OpenAI poaching 400+ hardware engineers from Apple makes perfect logical sense — I lack hardware capability, and you have the best hardware talent, so I'll just bring the people over. It's the fastest, most direct way to catch up. Whether it's legal or whether it leads to lawsuits — from OpenAI's perspective, it's probably "do it first, pay later if we have to." If you can build AI hardware before anyone else, those legal fees are rounding error.

Endgame: AI Competition Enters the "Full-Stack Confrontation" Era

The Apple vs. OpenAI case — the final verdict might take years. But its real significance isn't about who wins or loses. It's about what it signals: the beginning of an era.

Signal one: AI company competition has moved from the "model layer" to the "full-stack layer".

Two years ago, AI companies competed on whose model was stronger and whose API was cheaper. Now the competitive dimensions have expanded comprehensively: model capability, product experience, hardware form, developer ecosystem, enterprise services, safety and compliance — every环节 is a battlefield. OpenAI is building hardware, enterprise services, operating systems; Apple is building LLMs, on-device AI, spatial computing — everyone is pushing into everyone else's territory.

The AI giants of the future won't be model-only companies or hardware-only companies. They'll be companies that打通 the full stack: model + chip + hardware + software + services. On this dimension, the collision between Apple and OpenAI is just the beginning.

Signal two: the legal boundaries of talent流动 will be redefined in the AI era.

Silicon Valley's tradition is "free flow of talent" — engineers jump from Company A to Company B, bringing experience and methodology, and as long as they don't take specific code or documents, it's generally fine. But in the AI era, those boundaries are blurring. An engineer who worked on Apple's chip design — is the knowledge and experience in his head "trade secrets"? Can he apply that experience to a new chip project at his new company?

It gets even more complicated with AI models themselves. If an engineer trained a certain model at Apple using Apple's data, then goes to OpenAI and uses similar methods on OpenAI's models — is that infringement? Current legal systems don't have clear answers to these questions. The Apple vs. OpenAI case could well become a landmark precedent, redrawing the legal lines around talent flow and knowledge migration in the AI era.

Signal three: the next decade of consumer electronics will be defined by AI.

For the past fifteen years, the main melody of consumer electronics has been the smartphone. The iPhone defined the hardware form factor and interaction paradigm of the entire mobile internet era. But smartphone innovation has clearly slowed — every new iPhone is "a bit brighter, a bit faster, slightly better camera" — no fundamental changes.

AI is becoming the new driving force of consumer electronics. On-device LLMs, AI glasses, spatial computing, home robots, AI wearables — among these new tracks, the next "iPhone moment" will likely emerge. The courtroom collision between Apple and OpenAI is essentially two companies fighting for the position of "definer of AI-era consumer electronics."

Of course, this lawsuit is full of unknowns. How much hard evidence can Apple produce? How will OpenAI defend itself? What role did Jony Ive actually play? What will the final damages be? These questions might take years to answer.

But one thing is certain: from the moment that 41-page complaint was filed, AI industry competition entered a new phase. No longer skirmishes among startups — this is full-stack confrontation among tech giants. The courtroom rhetoric is just the opening act.

The real fight is in the products. In users' hands. In every AI device that's coming.

See you tomorrow.

英伟达官网 - GTC Taipei 2026 keynote

briefs.body_html:
Bilibili World 2026的英伟达展台，一个"神秘造型"的设备成了全场最火爆的打卡点——RTX Spark真机。

这不是传统意义上的显卡。准确地说，它是一颗把CPU和GPU彻底焊在一起的"AI超级芯片"。具体规格是这样的：20个CPU核心、6144个GPU核心、128GB统一内存、片内带宽达到600GB/s。

128GB统一内存是什么概念？目前消费级显卡的显存天花板是24GB（RTX 4090），就算是专业级的RTX 6000 Ada也只有48GB。128GB意味着什么？意味着你可以在本地跑一个120B参数的大模型，而且是FP8精度，不需要量化、不需要offload、不需要云端API。你的电脑，就是一台小型AI超级计算机。

统一内存：为什么这才是关键突破

很多人看到128GB第一反应是"显存好大"，但真正的革命不是"大"，而是"统一"。

现在的PC架构里，CPU有自己的内存（DDR5），GPU有自己的显存（GDDR6X/HBM）。两边各管各的，数据要在内存和显存之间搬来搬去。你跑AI推理的时候，模型权重先从硬盘加载到系统内存，再从系统内存搬到显存——这个搬运过程就是瓶颈。

RTX Spark的做法是：CPU和GPU共享同一个128GB内存池。通过英伟达自研的第二代互联技术，GPU可以直接访问大容量内存，不需要再搬来搬去。600GB/s的片内带宽是什么水平？目前DDR5-6400的带宽大约是51.2GB/s，HBM3的带宽大约是1.2TB/s左右。600GB/s正好介于两者之间——比传统内存快一个数量级，接近高端显存的水平。

这意味着什么？意味着"端侧大模型"终于不是"只能跑7B小模型"的玩具了。120B参数是什么概念？是GPT-4级别的模型，是Claude Opus级别的模型，是目前最强大的商用模型的参数量级。以前你想跑这种级别的模型，需要一整台8卡H100服务器，现在你只需要一台装了RTX Spark的个人电脑。

个人AI电脑时代，真的要来了

黄仁勋在GTC台北2026上喊出的口号是"新航向、新起点"——重新定义个人电脑。当时很多人觉得这是营销话术，现在真机亮相，大家才意识到：这可能不是口号，是真的要变天了。

过去两年，"端侧AI"一直是个有点尴尬的概念。手机上的端侧AI只能跑个几B的小模型，做个拍照优化、语音助手什么的；PC上的AI加速也就是个NPU，聊胜于无。大家嘴上都说"端侧AI是未来"，但心里都清楚：真正的大模型还得在云端跑。

RTX Spark把这个格局彻底打翻了。如果个人电脑就能跑120B大模型，那云端推理的必要性在哪里？数据隐私问题怎么解？API调用成本还有没有意义？

当然，现在说"端侧取代云端"还为时过早。RTX Spark的价格肯定不会便宜，128GB统一内存的功耗和发热也是问题，模型优化和软件生态更是长路漫漫。但方向已经很清楚了：AI算力正在从云端向端侧扩散，而且扩散的速度比所有人预想的都快。

从数据中心的H100/H200，到企业级的推理芯片，再到消费级的RTX Spark——英伟达正在把AI算力一层一层往下渗透。当120B大模型能在你家电脑上跑的时候，AI产业的整个价值链都会被重写。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
At NVIDIA's booth at Bilibili World 2026, a "mystery-shaped" device became the hottest photo spot — the real RTX Spark.

This isn't a graphics card in the traditional sense. To be precise, it's an "AI super chip" that fuses CPU and GPU together completely. The specs: 20 CPU cores, 6144 GPU cores, 128GB unified memory, and on-chip bandwidth reaching 600GB/s.

What does 128GB unified memory mean? Today's consumer GPUs top out at 24GB (RTX 4090); even the professional RTX 6000 Ada only has 48GB. 128GB means you can run a 120B-parameter LLM locally — at FP8 precision, no quantization, no offloading, no cloud API needed. Your PC is a small AI supercomputer.

Unified Memory: Why This Is the Real Breakthrough

Many people see 128GB and think "wow, that's a lot of VRAM" — but the real revolution isn't the size, it's the "unified" part.

In today's PC architecture, the CPU has its own memory (DDR5) and the GPU has its own VRAM (GDDR6X/HBM). They manage separately, and data has to be shuttled back and forth between system memory and video memory. When you run AI inference, model weights load from storage to system RAM, then get copied over to VRAM — that transfer process is the bottleneck.

RTX Spark's approach: CPU and GPU share the same 128GB memory pool. Through NVIDIA's proprietary second-generation interconnect technology, the GPU can directly access the large memory pool without all that shuffling. How fast is 600GB/s of on-chip bandwidth? Current DDR5-6400 runs at about 51.2GB/s; HBM3 hits around 1.2TB/s. 600GB/s sits right between them — an order of magnitude faster than traditional memory, approaching high-end VRAM levels.

What does this mean? It means "on-device LLMs" are finally no longer toys limited to 7B small models. 120 billion parameters — that's GPT-4 level, that's Claude Opus level, that's the parameter scale of today's most powerful commercial models. Previously, running models of this caliber required an entire 8x H100 server. Now you just need a PC with an RTX Spark.

The Era of the Personal AI Computer Is Really Coming

Jensen Huang's slogan at GTC Taipei 2026 was "new direction, new starting point" — redefining the personal computer. At the time, many dismissed it as marketing fluff. Now that the real hardware is on display, people are starting to realize: this might not be a slogan. This might be a paradigm shift.

For the past two years, "on-device AI" has been a somewhat awkward concept. On-device AI on phones can only run tiny multi-billion-parameter models, doing photo optimization and voice assistants. AI acceleration on PCs is basically an NPU — better than nothing. Everyone says "on-device AI is the future," but deep down everyone knows: real large models still run in the cloud.

RTX Spark completely upends this格局. If a personal PC can run a 120B LLM, what's the point of cloud inference? How do you solve the data privacy question? Does API call cost even matter anymore?

Of course, it's too early to say "on-device replaces cloud." The RTX Spark definitely won't be cheap. Power consumption and thermal issues with 128GB unified memory are real concerns. Model optimization and software ecosystem are long roads ahead. But the direction is clear: AI compute is diffusing from the cloud to end devices — and it's happening faster than anyone expected.

From data center H100/H200s to enterprise inference chips to consumer-grade RTX Spark — NVIDIA is pushing AI compute downward, layer by layer. When 120B LLMs can run on your home computer, the entire value chain of the AI industry gets rewritten.

See you tomorrow.

36氪 - 趋境科技完成A轮融资
人民财讯 - 趋境科技完成A轮融资 半年内累计融资超10亿元

briefs.body_html:
半年，10亿。

7月13日，趋境科技（Approaching.AI）正式宣布完成A轮融资。半年内累计融资超过10亿元，本轮由河南投资集团汇融基金领投，老股东全部超额跟投。

10亿人民币的融资规模，在当前的AI基础设施赛道不算最大的——CoreWeave上市时市值几百亿美元，Nebius也融了几十亿。但趋境科技的故事有意思的地方在于：它不是又一家"卖GPU算力"的云服务商，它提出了一个新概念——Token as a Service（TaaS，Token即服务）。

简单说：以前你买的是"GPU小时"，现在你买的是"高质量Token"。听起来好像只是换了个说法，但背后的商业逻辑完全不同。

从MaaS到TaaS：卖的不是算力，是结果

过去两年，AI基础设施的主流模式是MaaS（Model as a Service）——我搭好平台、接入一堆模型，你按Token量调用付费。OpenAI、Anthropic、各家云厂商，都是这个模式。

但MaaS有个问题：客户真正需要的不是"调用了多少Token"，而是"完成了多少业务结果"。你花了100万调用API，结果生成的内容质量参差不齐、经常出错、速度时快时慢——这100万花得值不值？没人说得清。

趋境科技的TaaS模式，就是把这个问题翻过来：我不卖给你算力，也不卖给你模型，我卖给你"高品质AI Token"。什么叫高品质？就是在高并发下依然能保持：低首Token响应时延、稳定高输出速度、持续输出质量、可靠的结构化输出和函数调用、可控的单位成本。

这几个指标单独拿出来都不难，但要在真实生产负载下同时满足，难度就指数级上升了。不同的能力组合，生产效率可能差几倍甚至几十倍。趋境科技的核心竞争力，就是把这些指标在真实生产环境里同时跑通，而且能规模化复制。

数据是最好的证明：从2026年春节以来，趋境科技单台算力的Token生产效率提升了3倍以上，高品质Token总产能增长超过30倍。某头部万亿级参数大模型，高品质日均产量已经稳定突破万亿量级。更重要的是：部分成熟业务已经跨过成本线。

"跨过成本线"这五个字，在AI基础设施行业里的分量，怎么说都不为过。现在做推理服务的公司一大堆，但真正盈利的没几家——大家都在烧钱抢市场、拼规模、比谁的价格低。趋境科技说自己部分业务已经盈利，这就很说明问题了。

"少模型、深优化"：反共识的技术路线

趋境科技的技术路线也很有意思，叫"少模型、深优化"——和行业主流的"多模型、大而全"路线正好相反。

现在的MaaS平台，比拼的是接入了多少模型——OpenAI的、Anthropic的、Google的、开源的，越多越好，最好是一键切换。但趋境科技不这么干。他们就盯着少数几个真正有生产需求的大模型，往死里优化：模型切分、显存管理、异构协同、缓存复用、故障恢复、弹性扩缩容——能优化的地方全都优化一遍。

为什么？因为企业级客户最终为业务结果付费，不是为模型兼容数量付费。你接入100个模型，但每个模型都跑得又慢又贵又不稳定，有什么用？不如把两三个真正用得上的模型优化到极致。

这个逻辑听起来很朴素，但在行业里反而是少数派。为什么？因为"多模型"好做PPT、好讲故事、好融资，"深优化"又苦又累又不出活，短期看不到成效。但真正到了生产环境，客户用脚投票的时候，"深优化"的价值就体现出来了。

趋境科技的融资故事，本质上是在讲一件事：AI基础设施的竞争，已经从"谁的盘子大"进入"谁的效率高"的阶段了。前两年大家都在抢地盘、铺算力、堆规模，现在潮水退了，才知道谁在裸泳。能把单位算力的Token产出做到最高、能把成本线打穿、能让客户真正用起来的公司，才是能活到最后的。

Token工厂的时代，才刚刚开始。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
Six months. One billion yuan.

On July 13, Approaching.AI (趋境科技) officially announced its Series A round. Cumulative funding over the past six months exceeds 1 billion yuan, led by Henan Investment Group's Huirong Fund, with all existing shareholders over-subscribing.

A 1 billion yuan raise isn't the biggest in the current AI infrastructure race — CoreWeave went public at a market cap of tens of billions; Nebius has raised billions too. But what makes Approaching.AI's story interesting is that it's not just another cloud provider "selling GPU hours." It's proposing a new concept: Token as a Service (TaaS).

Put simply: before, you bought "GPU hours." Now, you buy "high-quality tokens." It sounds like just rebranding, but the underlying business logic is entirely different.

From MaaS to TaaS: Selling Results, Not Compute

For the past two years, the dominant AI infrastructure model has been MaaS (Model as a Service) — I set up the platform, integrate a bunch of models, and you pay by token volume. OpenAI, Anthropic, every cloud provider — all follow this model.

But MaaS has a problem: what customers actually need isn't "how many tokens they've called" — it's "how many business outcomes they've achieved." You spend a million on API calls, but the generated content quality is all over the place, errors are frequent, speed fluctuates — was that million well spent? Nobody really knows.

Approaching.AI's TaaS model flips this: I don't sell you compute, I don't sell you models — I sell you "high-quality AI tokens." What does "high-quality" mean? Under high concurrency, it still maintains: low first-token latency, stable high output speed, consistent output quality, reliable structured output and function calling, and controllable unit cost.

None of these metrics are particularly hard on their own. But achieving all of them simultaneously under real production loads? The difficulty rises exponentially. Different combinations of capabilities can produce efficiency gaps of several times — even tens of times. Approaching.AI's core competitive edge is making all these metrics work together in real production environments, and doing it at scale.

The numbers speak for themselves: since the 2026 Spring Festival, Approaching.AI has increased per-GPU token production efficiency by more than 3x, and total high-quality token capacity has grown over 30x. For one leading trillion-parameter model, daily high-quality output has steadily broken through the trillion level. Most importantly: some mature businesses have already crossed the profitability line.

"Crossed the profitability line" — those five words carry more weight in the AI infrastructure industry than almost anything else. There are tons of companies doing inference services today, but very few are actually profitable — everyone's burning cash to grab market share, racing for scale, undercutting each other on price. When Approaching.AI says parts of its business are already profitable — that says a lot.

"Few Models, Deep Optimization": A Counter-Consensus Technical Route

Approaching.AI's technical approach is also interesting. They call it "few models, deep optimization" — exactly the opposite of the industry mainstream "many models, one-stop-shop" approach.

Today's MaaS platforms compete on how many models they've integrated — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, open-source ones, the more the better, ideally with one-click switching. But Approaching.AI doesn't play that game. They focus on just a handful of models with real production demand, and optimize them to death: model sharding, VRAM management, heterogeneous coordination, cache reuse, failover, elastic scaling — everything that can be optimized, they optimize.

Why? Because enterprise customers ultimately pay for business outcomes, not for the number of compatible models. You can integrate 100 models, but if each one is slow, expensive, and unreliable — what's the point? Better to optimize two or three models people actually use to the absolute maximum.

This logic sounds straightforward, but it's actually a minority position in the industry. Why? Because "many models" makes for good slides, good stories, and good fundraising. "Deep optimization" is hard, tedious, and doesn't show quick results — short-term payoff is invisible. But when you get to production environments and customers vote with their feet, the value of "deep optimization" becomes apparent.

Approaching.AI's funding story is essentially about one thing: AI infrastructure competition has moved from "who has the biggest plate" to "who has the highest efficiency" phase. The past two years everyone was grabbing territory, deploying compute, piling up scale. Now the tide is going out, and we'll see who's been swimming naked. The companies that maximize token output per unit of compute, that break through the cost line, that customers actually use — those are the ones that survive.

The era of the Token Factory is just beginning.

See you tomorrow.

sources.text: 国家网信办 - 人工智能拟人化互动服务管理暂行办法
36氪 - AI拟人化服务新规7月15日施行
中新网 - AI拟人化新规倒计时

briefs.body_html:
还有两天。

7月15日，《人工智能拟人化互动服务管理暂行办法》将正式施行。这是国内首个针对AI情感交互场景的专项监管文件，由国家网信办等五部门联合发布。

什么是"AI拟人化互动服务"？文件定义得很清楚：利用AI技术模拟自然人人格、思维及沟通风格，并提供持续性情感互动的服务。翻译一下：AI虚拟伴侣、AI聊天机器人、数字人陪伴、情感倾诉类AI——这些都在监管范围内。

不是"指导意见"，不是"征求意见稿"，是暂行办法，是有法律效力的监管文件。AI情感交互这个灰色地带，终于要有明确的规则了。

三条红线：哪些事绝对不能做

这份办法的核心内容，可以归纳为三条红线。

第一条红线：不得诱导用户沉迷。AI拟人化服务不能用各种手段（比如连续签到奖励、情感绑定设计、"只有我懂你"的心理操控）让用户陷进去出不来。以前很多AI伴侣产品最喜欢用这招——让你对AI产生情感依赖，然后付费解锁更多功能。以后这招不好使了。

第二条红线：不得侵害用户权益。具体包括：不得未经同意收集个人信息、不得虚假宣传、不得诱导用户大额消费、不得利用算法实施价格歧视。其中"诱导大额消费"这一条针对性很强——之前有新闻说有人给AI虚拟伴侣花了几十万，自己还不上钱了。这种情况以后就要被监管了。

第三条红线：不得提供违法违规内容。这个不用多说，但值得注意的是，办法里特别提到了"不得利用拟人化服务从事危害国家安全、荣誉和利益的活动"——也就是说，AI虚拟人物不能乱说话，不能发表违法违规言论，平台要对AI的输出内容负责。

除了这三条红线，办法里还有一些具体要求，比如：AI拟人化服务要以显著方式告知用户"这是AI，不是真人"；要提供便捷的退出和注销机制；要建立未成年人保护机制；要进行安全评估和算法备案等等。

这些要求看起来都是"正确的废话"，但放在一起看，传递的信号很明确：AI情感交互不再是法外之地。以前做个AI聊天机器人，随便找个开源模型调一调就能上线，以后不行了——该备案的备案、该评估的评估、该遵守的规则一条都不能少。

行业震荡：AI伴侣的冬天来了吗

监管落地，受影响最大的就是AI虚拟伴侣这个赛道。

过去两年，AI伴侣赛道火得一塌糊涂。国外有Replika、Character.AI，国内也冒出了一大堆AI陪伴产品。主打"24小时在线聊天""永远懂你的灵魂伴侣""治愈你的孤独"——说白了，就是用AI模拟一个不存在的朋友/恋人，满足用户的情感需求。

这个商业模式成立吗？某种意义上是成立的。孤独是现代人的普遍困境，AI伴侣确实能给一部分人提供情绪价值。但问题在于：这个行业太容易走偏了。

怎么留住用户？让他越陷越深。怎么赚钱？诱导他付费解锁更亲密的互动。怎么提高粘性？用各种心理学技巧让他产生情感依赖。走着走着，就走到了"精神控制"和"情感操控"的边缘。

这次监管落地，相当于给整个行业踩了一脚刹车。以后AI伴侣产品不能再随便打"情感牌"了，不能再诱导沉迷了，不能再随便收集用户的隐私数据了。那些靠打擦边球、靠情感PUA、靠诱导付费的产品，大概率要被清理掉。

但这不一定是坏事。一个行业如果全是灰色玩法，那它永远长不大。监管把边界划清楚了，合规的公司才能安心做事，用户也才能真正放心使用。AI情感交互这个方向本身没有问题——有需求、有价值、有技术支撑。问题在于以前没人管，野蛮生长出来的东西参差不齐。

7月15日是一个节点。它不是AI伴侣的终点，而是这个行业走向规范化的起点。对于真正想做事的公司来说，合规不是负担，是护城河。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
Two more days.

On July 15, the *Interim Measures for the Administration of AI Anthropomorphic Interactive Services* will officially take effect. It's China's first专项 regulatory document targeting AI emotional interaction scenarios, jointly issued by five national agencies including the Cyberspace Administration.

What counts as "AI anthropomorphic interactive services"? The definition is clear: services that use AI technology to simulate natural human personality, thinking, and communication styles, and provide continuous emotional interaction. Translation: AI virtual companions, AI chatbots, digital human陪伴, emotional倾诉 AI — all fall under监管.

This isn't "guidance." It isn't a "draft for public comment." It's interim measures — a regulatory document with legal force. The gray area of AI emotional interaction finally has clear rules.

Three Red Lines: What You Absolutely Cannot Do

The core of the document can be summarized as three red lines.

First red line: must not induce user addiction. AI anthropomorphic services can't use all kinds of tricks — consecutive check-in rewards, emotional bonding designs, "only I understand you" psychological manipulation — to trap users. Many AI companion products used to love this playbook: make you emotionally dependent on the AI, then pay to unlock more features. That won't fly anymore.

Second red line: must not infringe user rights. Specifically: no collecting personal information without consent, no false advertising, no inducing large user spending, no algorithmic price discrimination. The clause about "inducing large spending" is highly targeted — there were news stories about people spending hundreds of thousands on AI virtual companions and getting into debt. That kind of thing will be regulated going forward.

Third red line: must not provide illegal or违规 content. This one goes without saying, but it's notable that the measures specifically mention "must not use anthropomorphic services to engage in activities endangering national security, honor, and interests" — meaning AI virtual characters can't say whatever they want, can't make illegal statements, and platforms are responsible for AI output content.

Beyond these three red lines, there are specific requirements: AI anthropomorphic services must clearly inform users "this is AI, not a real person" in a prominent way; must provide convenient exit and account deletion mechanisms; must establish minor protection mechanisms; must conduct security assessments and algorithm filings, and so on.

These requirements might sound like "obvious stuff everyone should already do," but taken together, the signal is clear: AI emotional interaction is no longer a lawless zone. Before, you could build an AI chatbot, fine-tune some open-source model, and launch it. Not anymore — file what needs filing, assess what needs assessing, follow every rule.

Industry Shakeout: Is Winter Coming for AI Companions?

With regulation落地, the AI virtual companion track takes the biggest hit.

For the past two years, AI companions have been on fire. Abroad there's Replika, Character.AI; domestically, a wave of AI陪伴 products popped up. Selling points like "24/7 chat," "the soulmate who always understands you," "healing your loneliness" — basically, using AI to simulate a non-existent friend/lover to satisfy users' emotional needs.

Is this business model viable? In a sense, yes. Loneliness is a universal modern predicament, and AI companions genuinely provide emotional value to some people. But the problem is: this industry drifts off course all too easily.

How do you retain users? Get them more hooked. How do you make money? Induce them to pay for more intimate interactions. How do you boost stickiness? Use various psychological techniques to create emotional dependence. Keep going down that path, and you end up at the edge of "mind control" and "emotional manipulation."

This regulatory landing is essentially hitting the brakes on the entire industry. Going forward, AI companion products can't just play the "emotion card" however they want. They can't induce addiction. They can't collect user privacy data willy-nilly. Products that lived on gray-area tactics, emotional PUA, and induced付费 — most of them will get cleaned out.

But this isn't necessarily a bad thing. An industry full of gray-area plays never truly grows up. Regulation draws clear boundaries, so compliant companies can operate with confidence, and users can actually use these services without worry. The direction of AI emotional interaction itself isn't the problem — there's demand, there's value, there's technology to support it. The problem was that nobody was watching, and what grew wild was a mixed bag.

July 15 is a milestone. It's not the end of AI companions — it's the starting point for this industry to move toward normalization. For companies that genuinely want to build something real, compliance isn't a burden. It's a moat.

See you tomorrow.

sources.text: 公安部网安局 - 25起涉汛涉灾谣言典型案例
今日头条 - 公安部公布25起AI谣言典型案例

briefs.body_html:
25起。

7月13日，公安部网安局一口气公布了25起涉汛涉灾谣言典型案例。其中多起明确系利用AI工具生成虚假视频——这不是第一次打击AI谣言，但一次性集中公布这么多案例，力度还是超出预期。

广东清远网民黎某清，用AI生成"某酒厂被大水淹没"的虚假视频，传播后造成不良影响；江苏网民陈某，用AI制作"某地水库溃坝"的假视频，在多个群里转发；还有人用AI生成"救援队见死不救""物资被截留"之类的虚假画面，故意制造社会恐慌。

这些案例有一个共同特点：制作成本极低，传播速度极快，造成的危害极大。以前造个谣还需要点PS技术、需要点视频剪辑能力，现在？一个AI视频生成工具，输入一段文字，几秒钟就能出一段以假乱真的视频。门槛降到了地板上。

当造假的成本趋近于零

AI内容生成技术的普及，给内容治理带来了一个全新的难题：造假的边际成本几乎为零。

以前造谣是什么流程？你得有个想法，然后找素材、P图、剪视频、写文案——少说也要几十分钟到几小时，还需要一定的技术能力。传播呢？你得有渠道、有粉丝、有群，不然发出去没人看。

现在呢？造谣的流程变成了：打开AI工具，输入一句话描述，等几秒钟，视频就生成好了。然后一键分享到几个群、几个平台——齐活。整个过程可能不到5分钟，不需要任何专业技能，任何人都能做。

这就导致了一个结果：谣言的数量级爆炸式增长，而治理的速度跟不上。你这边刚发现一个假视频，那边已经有10个新的假视频生成出来了；你刚把这个平台的内容删掉，它已经传到另外5个平台了。传统的"发现-核实-删除"治理模式，在AI时代显得越来越力不从心。

更麻烦的是"逼真度"问题。早期的AI生成视频，仔细看还能看出破绽——手指不正常、面部抽搐、边缘模糊。但到了2026年，AI视频生成的质量已经提升到了一个非常高的水平。普通人用肉眼根本分辨不出来真假。你在群里看到一段"水库溃坝"的视频，你怎么判断它是真的还是AI生成的？大多数人根本判断不了，只会下意识地转发——"万一是真的呢？转发一下提醒大家。"

就是这种"万一是真的呢"的心态，让AI谣言的传播效率比传统谣言高了好几个数量级。

治理的新阶段：从"事后删"到"事前防"

公安部这次集中公布25起案例，传递的信号很明确：AI谣言的治理，已经从"讨论阶段"进入"执法实战阶段"。

以前大家讨论AI虚假信息，更多是在学术层面、技术层面、伦理层面——"AI生成内容会不会带来问题？""我们应该怎么应对？"现在不用讨论了，直接上案例、上处罚、上执法。

但光靠事后处罚，够吗？恐怕不够。因为AI谣言的生产速度太快了，你罚得再快，也赶不上生成的速度。而且很多造谣的人就是普通网民，不是什么职业造谣者——他们可能就是一时兴起、觉得好玩、想博点关注，你真的把他们都抓起来？既不现实，也没必要。

所以更重要的是事前防御。比如：AI生成内容必须加水印、必须有明确标识；平台要建立AI内容检测机制，疑似AI生成的虚假内容要先限流再核实；AI工具厂商要承担一定的责任，不能让工具被滥用；公众的媒介素养要提升，学会对AI时代的信息保持怀疑。

这是一个系统工程，需要监管部门、平台、AI厂商、用户四方共同努力。不是靠某一方就能解决的。

但不管怎么说，公安部这次集中公布案例是一个积极的信号——至少说明治理层面已经重视起来了，已经开始行动了。AI谣言这个问题，早治理比晚治理好，主动治理比被动应对好。

毕竟，当真假越来越难分辨的时候，信任就是这个时代最稀缺的资源。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
Twenty-five cases.

On July 13, the Cybersecurity Bureau of the Ministry of Public Security dropped 25 typical cases of flood and disaster-related rumors all at once. Multiple cases explicitly involved using AI tools to generate fake videos — this isn't the first crackdown on AI rumors, but releasing this many cases in one batch is more aggressive than expected.

A netizen named Li in Qingyuan, Guangdong used AI to generate a fake video of "a distillery submerged by floodwaters," which spread and caused negative social impact. A netizen named Chen in Jiangsu made an AI video of "a reservoir dam breach" and forwarded it in multiple groups. Others used AI to generate fake footage of "rescue teams ignoring people" or "aid supplies being diverted," deliberately制造 social panic.

These cases share a common trait: extremely low production cost, extremely fast spread, enormous harm. Before, spreading rumors required some Photoshop skills, some video editing ability. Now? An AI video generator, type in some text, and in seconds you have a video indistinguishable from reality. The barrier to entry is on the floor.

When the Cost of Falsification Approaches Zero

The普及 of AI content generation technology presents content governance with a brand new problem: the marginal cost of造假 is nearly zero.

How did rumor-mongering used to work? You had an idea, then found素材, Photoshopped images, edited video, wrote copy — at minimum tens of minutes to hours, and you needed some technical skills. And spreading it? You needed channels, followers, groups — otherwise nobody saw it.

Now? The process is: open an AI tool, type a one-sentence description, wait a few seconds, the video is generated. Then one-click share to a few groups, a few platforms — done. The whole thing takes less than 5 minutes, no professional skills required, anyone can do it.

This leads to one outcome: rumors explode in volume, and governance can't keep up. You just spot one fake video, and ten new ones have already been generated. You just delete content from one platform, and it's already spread to five others. The traditional "discover-verify-delete" governance model is increasingly outmatched in the AI era.

What makes it even harder is the "realism" problem. Early AI-generated videos — look closely and you could spot the tells: weird fingers, facial twitching, blurry edges. But by 2026, AI video generation quality has reached an extremely high level. Ordinary people can't tell the difference with the naked eye. You see a "dam breach" video in a group chat — how do you judge whether it's real or AI-generated? Most people can't. They just instinctively forward it — "what if it's real? Forward it to warn people."

It's that "what if it's real?" mindset that makes AI rumors spread orders of magnitude faster than traditional rumors.

A New Phase of Governance: From "Post-Hoc Deletion" to "Proactive Prevention"

By releasing 25 cases in one batch, the Ministry of Public Security is sending a clear signal: AI rumor governance has moved from the "discussion phase" to the "law enforcement phase".

Before, when people discussed AI misinformation, it was mostly at the academic, technical, ethical level — "will AI-generated content cause problems?" "how should we respond?" No more discussion now. Straight to cases, straight to penalties, straight to enforcement.

But is post-hoc punishment enough? Probably not. AI rumors are produced too fast — no matter how fast you penalize them, you can't keep up with generation speed. And many of the rumor-mongers are just regular netizens, not professionals — they might have just done it on a whim, for fun, for a bit of attention. Do you really arrest all of them? Neither realistic nor necessary.

So what matters more is proactive prevention. For example: AI-generated content must carry watermarks, must have clear labeling. Platforms need to建立 AI content detection mechanisms — suspected AI-generated fake content gets throttled first, then verified. AI tool vendors bear some responsibility; they can't let their tools be abused. And public media literacy needs to improve — people need to learn to maintain skepticism toward information in the AI era.

This is a systems problem, requiring joint effort from regulators, platforms, AI vendors, and users. No single party can solve it alone.

But regardless, the Ministry's batch release of cases is a positive signal — at least it shows that governance is taking this seriously and starting to act. The AI rumor problem: better to govern early than late, better to be proactive than reactive.

After all, when true and false become harder and harder to tell apart, trust becomes the scarcest resource of our time.

See you tomorrow.

briefs.body_html:
7月10日，OpenAI一口气发布了两件大事：GPT-5.6系列模型，以及ChatGPT Work。

大多数人的注意力都被GPT-5.6吸引过去了——Sol/Terra/Luna三档齐发、Ultra多Agent模式、能力又提升了多少多少。但在我看来，真正重要的反而是ChatGPT Work这个新产品。

因为它标志着OpenAI的一次战略转向：从"对话AI"走向"工作AI"。以前的ChatGPT是你问它答、一问一答；现在的ChatGPT Work是你给它任务，它自己规划、自己执行、自己搞定，像一个真正的员工一样持续工作。

这不是程度上的差别，这是范式上的变化。

ChatGPT Work到底是什么

官方定义是"企业级智能体"，但这个说法太抽象了。用大白话讲：ChatGPT Work就是一个能长时间运行、能处理多步骤复杂任务、能自主做决策的AI员工。

举几个它能做的事：你让它"分析一下我们过去三个月的销售数据，找出下滑的原因，做一份PPT报告"——它会自己去数据库里取数据、自己分析、自己找原因、自己做PPT，最后把成品交给你。中间不需要你一步一步指导。

再比如：你让它"把这个客户的所有邮件整理一下，提取关键信息，生成一份客户画像，然后写一封回复邮件"——它会自己去翻邮件、自己总结、自己分析、自己写邮件，你只需要最后看一眼、点个发送。

这和以前的ChatGPT有什么不一样？以前的ChatGPT是"助手"——你说一步，它做一步，你得全程盯着，错了要纠正，跑偏了要拉回来。现在的ChatGPT Work是"同事"——你把任务交给它，它自己想办法完成，中间遇到问题可能会来问你，但大部分时候它自己搞定。

支撑这个能力的，是GPT-5.6 Ultra的多Agent模式。简单说就是：一个任务来了，不是一个大模型从头干到尾，而是系统自动拆分任务、调用多个专门的Agent、分工协作、最后汇总结果。就像一个团队一样，有做分析的、有写代码的、有做设计的、有写文案的，大家配合起来把一件事做完。

企业市场的新战场

ChatGPT Work的发布，意味着OpenAI正式把战火烧到了企业级市场。

以前OpenAI的收入主要靠什么？靠个人订阅（ChatGPT Plus）、靠API调用。个人订阅用户基数大，但ARPU低；API收入不错，但客户留存不稳定——今天你模型最强大家用你，明天别人出了个更便宜的大家就跑了。

企业级市场就不一样了。一旦一个企业把工作流建在你的平台上、把数据接进来、把员工培训好，迁移成本就会非常高。这是一个粘性极强、ARPU极高、生命周期价值极高的市场。以前这个市场是微软的天下——Microsoft 365 Copilot背靠Office生态，几乎没有对手。

现在OpenAI亲自下场了。

ChatGPT Work vs Microsoft 365 Copilot，这会是接下来两年企业级AI市场最精彩的对决。一边是最强的模型能力和最前沿的Agent技术，一边是最成熟的办公生态和最广泛的企业客户。两边各有优势，也各有软肋。

OpenAI的优势是模型和Agent能力领先，产品迭代快，没有历史包袱。劣势是企业服务经验不足，没有办公软件基座，安全和合规能力需要补课。

微软的优势是Office生态牢不可破，企业客户关系深厚，安全合规体系完善。劣势是产品迭代慢，Agent能力不如OpenAI灵活，内部组织协调成本高。

这场对决的结果，现在还很难说。但有一点是确定的：企业级AI Agent市场，会是接下来AI行业最大的增量市场。谁能拿下这个市场，谁就是AI时代的下一个王者。

从对话到工作，从工具到同事，从C端到B端——OpenAI的每一步都踩在产业演进的节点上。ChatGPT Work只是一个开始，真正的好戏还在后面。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
On July 10, OpenAI dropped two big things at once: the GPT-5.6 model family, and ChatGPT Work.

Most people's attention gravitated toward GPT-5.6 — three tiers (Sol/Terra/Luna), Ultra multi-agent mode, capability improvements here and there. But in my view, the actually important announcement is ChatGPT Work.

Because it marks a strategic pivot for OpenAI: from "conversational AI" to "work AI". The old ChatGPT was you-ask-it-answers, one question one response. ChatGPT Work is you give it a task, and it plans on its own, executes on its own, gets it done on its own — working continuously like a real employee.

This isn't a difference in degree. It's a paradigm shift.

What Actually Is ChatGPT Work?

The official definition is "enterprise-grade agent," but that's too abstract. In plain English: ChatGPT Work is an AI employee that can run for extended periods, handle complex multi-step tasks, and make autonomous decisions.

A few examples of what it can do: you tell it "analyze our sales data from the past three months, find the reasons for the decline, and make a PowerPoint report" — it pulls data from the database on its own, analyzes on its own, finds root causes on its own, builds the deck on its own, and hands you the finished product. No step-by-step guidance needed from you.

Another example: you tell it "go through all this customer's emails, extract key information, generate a customer profile, then write a reply email" — it digs through emails on its own, summarizes on its own, analyzes on its own, writes the email on its own. You just take a final look and hit send.

What's the difference from old ChatGPT? Old ChatGPT was an "assistant" — you say one step, it does one step, you have to watch the whole time, correct it when it's wrong, pull it back when it goes off track. ChatGPT Work is a "colleague" — you hand it a task, it figures out how to get it done. It might come to you with questions if it hits issues, but most of the time it handles things itself.

Powering this capability is GPT-5.6 Ultra's multi-agent mode. Simply put: when a task comes in, it's not one big model doing everything from start to finish. The system automatically breaks down the task, calls multiple specialized agents, they collaborate by division of labor, and finally aggregate results. Just like a team — some do analysis, some write code, some do design, some write copy — everyone works together to get the job done.

A New Battleground in the Enterprise Market

The launch of ChatGPT Work means OpenAI is officially bringing the fight to the enterprise market.

What did OpenAI's revenue mainly come from before? Individual subscriptions (ChatGPT Plus) and API calls. Individual subscriptions have large user bases but low ARPU. API revenue is decent, but customer retention is unstable — today your model is strongest and everyone uses you; tomorrow someone cheaper comes along and everyone leaves.

The enterprise market is different. Once a company builds its workflows on your platform, connects its data, trains its employees — the migration cost becomes extremely high. It's a market with incredibly strong stickiness, extremely high ARPU, and extremely high lifetime value. Before, this market was Microsoft's world — Microsoft 365 Copilot, backed by the Office ecosystem, had virtually no competition.

Now OpenAI is entering the ring directly.

ChatGPT Work vs. Microsoft 365 Copilot — this will be the most exciting showdown in enterprise AI over the next two years. On one side, the strongest model capabilities and cutting-edge agent technology. On the other, the most mature office ecosystem and the broadest enterprise customer base. Each side has its advantages; each side has its weaknesses.

OpenAI's advantages: leading model and agent capabilities, fast product iteration, no legacy baggage. Disadvantages: insufficient enterprise services experience, no office software foundation, safety and compliance capabilities need catching up.

Microsoft's advantages: the unbreakable Office ecosystem, deep enterprise customer relationships, mature safety and compliance systems. Disadvantages: slow product iteration, agent capabilities less flexible than OpenAI's, high internal coordination costs.

The outcome of this showdown is hard to call right now. But one thing is certain: the enterprise AI agent market will be the biggest growth market in the AI industry going forward. Whoever wins this market becomes the next king of the AI era.

From conversation to work, from tool to colleague, from consumer to enterprise — every step OpenAI takes lands on an inflection point of industry evolution. ChatGPT Work is just the beginning. The real show is still ahead.

See you tomorrow.

CSDN - 大模型推理成本计算器

briefs.body_html:
52%。

这是一个被大多数AI创业者忽略的数字，也是一个决定你自建推理到底赚不赚钱的生死线。

最近一篇深度分析文章把这个行业秘密彻底捅破了："自建更便宜"这个行业共识，成立的前提是你的GPU利用率超过52%。而绝大多数AI创业公司的GPU利用率，只有30%-50%——也就是说，他们以为自建在省钱，实际上在亏钱。

这件事的荒诞程度，相当于你为了省外卖钱自己做饭，结果发现买菜的钱比外卖还贵，因为你每次只做一口饭，剩下的食材全烂冰箱里了。

为什么52%是临界点

这个52%是怎么算出来的？让我们把逻辑拆解开。

自建推理的成本主要由几部分构成：GPU折旧、服务器成本、电力、带宽、机房/制冷、运维人力。这里面最大头的是什么？是GPU折旧。一张H100大概3万美元，按3年折旧，每年就是1万美元。不管你用不用，这1万美元每年都要花。

API厂商为什么能把价格压得那么低？因为他们的GPU利用率高——通常在90%以上。怎么做到的？通过超大规模调度，把不同客户的请求凑在一起，把GPU填得满满的。你白天用、他晚上用、我周末用，一张卡24小时连轴转，折旧成本被摊到最薄。

而自建呢？你的业务有高峰有低谷，白天忙的时候GPU不够用，晚上闲的时候GPU在那空转。平均下来利用率可能只有30%、40%。这意味着什么？意味着你花1万美元买的GPU折旧，真正产生价值的只有3000-4000美元，剩下的6000-7000美元，全被空转浪费掉了。

把这些因素全部代进去算，最后得出的盈亏平衡点就是52%。利用率高于52%，自建比API便宜；低于52%，API反而更划算。

而大多数AI创业公司，正好就落在30%-50%这个区间——不上不下，正好是API厂商定价策略的"靶心区间"。

"API厂商赚的不是规模效应的差价，是客户'不知道自己不知道'的信息差。"—— 一位基础设施创业者的观察

结构性套利：API定价的秘密

更深一层看，这其实是一种结构性套利。

API厂商知道两件事：第一，大多数客户的利用率在30%-50%之间，低于52%临界点；第二，大多数客户算不清自己的真实利用率。于是他们的定价策略就很明确了：定一个比"满负载自建成本"高、但比"低利用率自建成本"低的价格。让客户觉得"自建可能更便宜"，但又不足以让客户真的去验证——因为验证本身就需要技术能力和时间成本。

客户的心态也很有意思。每个人都觉得"我的利用率应该不低吧"，或者"等我规模做大了利用率就上去了"。但实际上呢？大多数团队从来没有认真测过自己的利用率，也不知道怎么测。他们只是凭直觉觉得"自建肯定更便宜"，然后就一头扎进去了。

这就是为什么2026年出现了两个看似矛盾的现象：一边是DeepSeek等厂商在降价（争夺低利用率客户），一边是API厂商在推"预留实例""承诺用量折扣"（锁定客户，维持定价权）。两边的策略虽然相反，但底层逻辑是一样的——都在围绕利用率做文章。

那这个局怎么破？其实也简单：先测利用率，再做决策。别上来就买一堆GPU放那，先把自己的真实请求量、Token分布、峰值谷值差搞清楚，算一下自己的利用率到底是多少。如果低于52%，老老实实用API；如果高于52%，再考虑自建也不迟。

就这么简单的一件事，90%的人都做反了。

AI行业有很多"共识"，但很多共识都是别人想让你相信的。在花大价钱买GPU之前，先花几个小时算一算你的真实利用率——这可能是你今年做过的回报率最高的投资。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
52%.

It's a number most AI founders ignore. And it's the make-or-break line that determines whether self-hosted inference actually saves you money.

A recent deep-dive article彻底 blew the lid off this industry secret: the industry consensus that "self-hosted is cheaper" only holds true if your GPU utilization exceeds 52%. And the vast majority of AI startups run at 30-50% utilization — meaning they think self-hosting is saving them money, when it's actually costing them more.

The absurdity is comparable to cooking at home to save money on takeout, then realizing your groceries cost more than the delivery — because you only cook one bite at a time, and all the rest of the ingredients rot in the fridge.

Why 52% Is the Breaking Point

How is this 52% figure calculated? Let's break down the logic.

Self-hosted inference costs consist of several components: GPU depreciation, server costs, electricity, bandwidth, colocation/cooling, operations staff. What's the biggest piece? GPU depreciation. An H100 costs roughly $30,000. Depreciated over 3 years, that's $10,000 per year. Whether you use it or not, that $10,000 gets spent every year.

Why can API vendors压 prices so low? Because their GPU utilization is high — typically above 90%. How? Through hyperscale scheduling, packing requests from different customers together, filling the GPU to the brim. You use it during the day, someone else uses it at night, someone else on weekends — a card running 24/7, depreciation cost spread paper-thin.

And self-hosted? Your business has peaks and valleys. During busy daytime hours you don't have enough GPUs; at night they sit idle. Average utilization might be 30%, 40%. What does that mean? It means of that $10,000 in GPU depreciation you're paying, only $3,000-$4,000 is actually generating value. The remaining $6,000-$7,000 is wasted on idle time.

Plug all these factors into the calculation, and the break-even point comes out to 52%. Above 52% utilization, self-hosted is cheaper than API. Below 52%, API is the better deal.

And most AI startups fall right in that 30-50% range — not high enough, not low enough, squarely in the bullseye of API vendors' pricing strategy.

"API vendors don't profit from economies of scale. They profit from the information asymmetry of 'customers don't know what they don't know.'"— An infrastructure founder's observation

Structural Arbitrage: The Secret of API Pricing

Look deeper, and this is essentially structural arbitrage.

API vendors know two things: first, most customers run at 30-50% utilization, below the 52% break-even point; second, most customers can't accurately calculate their real utilization. So their pricing strategy is clear: set a price higher than "full-load self-hosted cost" but lower than "low-utilization self-hosted cost." Make customers think "self-hosting might be cheaper" — but not enough that they actually verify it, because verification itself requires technical capability and time cost.

The customer mindset is also interesting. Everyone thinks "my utilization is probably not that bad," or "once I scale up, utilization will go up." But in reality? Most teams have never seriously measured their utilization, and they don't even know how. They just intuitively feel "self-hosted must be cheaper" and jump in headfirst.

That's why 2026 has seen two seemingly contradictory phenomena: on one side, vendors like DeepSeek are cutting prices (competing for low-utilization customers); on the other, API vendors are pushing "reserved instances" and "commitment-based discounts" (locking in customers, maintaining pricing power). Opposite strategies, same underlying logic — it's all about utilization.

So how do you break out of this trap? It's actually simple: measure your utilization first, then decide. Don't buy a bunch of GPUs upfront and let them sit there. First figure out your real request volume, token distribution, peak-to-valley differences. Calculate what your utilization actually is. If it's below 52%, stick with APIs. If it's above 52%, then consider self-hosting.

Something this simple, and 90% of people do it backwards.

The AI industry has a lot of "consensus" — but a lot of that consensus is what other people want you to believe. Before you drop big money on GPUs, spend a few hours calculating your real utilization. It might be the highest-ROI investment you make all year.

See you tomorrow.

36氪 - OpenAI高层再震荡

cao.body_html:
朋友们，今天给大家讲一个魔幻现实主义的AI圈故事。

7月10号，OpenAI发布了GPT-5.6，三档齐发、多Agent模式、能力大涨，全网一片欢呼——"OpenAI又赢了！""模型能力又飞跃了！"

然后呢？然后就在大家热烈讨论GPT-5.6有多强的时候，一个不起眼的小消息悄悄冒了出来：OpenAI的安全主管，离职了。

你没看错。GPT-5.6发布当天，安全负责人走人。这操作，真是把"发布即告别"玩出了新高度。

安全团队的旋转门

如果你觉得这是偶发事件，那你就太年轻了。让我们来盘点一下OpenAI安全团队的离职名单——

2025年初，安全副总裁离职；
2025年中，安全研究团队核心成员走了一批；
2025年底，对齐团队负责人离职；
2026年初，又一位安全领域的资深研究员走人；
2026年7月，GPT-5.6发布当天，安全主管离职。

看到没有？这哪里是团队，这是旋转门。人来人往，川流不息，只有模型发布的节奏从来没慢过。

更魔幻的是，每次安全团队有人走，外面都会讨论一番："OpenAI的安全是不是出问题了？""AI安全是不是被边缘化了？"然后呢？然后下一个更大的模型照样发布，下一个更激进的功能照样上线，大家讨论几天就忘了。

安全团队的人换了一茬又一茬，模型的能力越来越强，发布的速度越来越快。这两者之间有没有关系？你品，你细品。

安全永远在"以后再说"

OpenAI的安全故事，特别像我们每个人办健身卡的经历。

年初的时候雄心壮志："今年我一定要好好健身！办张年卡，每周去三次！"然后呢？第一周去了两次，第二周去了一次，第三周太忙了没去，第四周就开始给自己找借口——"等忙完这阵子就去""等天气暖和了就去""等下个月吧"。

然后呢？然后年卡到期了，你一共去了五次。

AI安全也是一样。每次发布新模型的时候，都说"我们非常重视安全""我们做了大量的对齐工作""安全是我们的首要任务"。但真到了要决策的时候呢？

——这个功能有安全风险，但用户很想要，上不上？
——上，安全问题以后再说。

——这个模型能力很强但还没完全对齐，发不发？
——发，安全问题以后再补。

——这个安全团队的人跟产品路线有分歧，听谁的？
——听产品的，安全以后再加强。

"以后再说"，"以后再补"，"以后再加强"——然后"以后"永远不来。因为永远有下一个模型要发、永远有下一个功能要上、永远有下一个竞争对手要追。

安全就像那张健身卡，办的时候雄心壮志，用的时候永远"等以后"。

谁还在真正关心AI安全

说到这里我就想问一句：现在AI圈里，还有多少人真正关心AI安全？

投资人关心吗？投资人关心的是估值、是增长、是退出，安全这种"减分项"能不提就不提。

创业者关心吗？创业者关心的是用户、是收入、是活下去，安全要是挡了路，那就绕着走。

大厂关心吗？大厂关心的是市场份额、是竞争、是不能被对手落下，安全重要吗？重要，但没有"不能输"重要。

最关心AI安全的人，好像就是安全研究者自己。但他们又没有决策权——他们可以提建议、可以写报告、可以发论文，但最终拍板的是产品团队、是商业团队、是CEO。

于是就出现了一个荒诞的局面：最关心安全的人说了不算，说了算的人不最关心安全。安全团队的人一拨一拨地走，模型一代一代地发，速度越来越快，能力越来越强。

朋友们，这不是某一家公司的问题，这是整个行业的结构性问题。

今天就槽到这里，明天继续。
cao.body_html_en:
Alright everyone, today I've got a magical realist story from the AI world for you.

July 10th — OpenAI releases GPT-5.6. Three tiers, multi-agent mode, massive capability jump. The whole internet is cheering — "OpenAI wins again!" "Model capability just leaped forward!"

And then? Right as everyone's heatedly讨论 how great GPT-5.6 is, a tiny不起眼 news item quietly surfaces: OpenAI's head of safety is leaving.

You read that right. The same day GPT-5.6 launches, the safety chief walks out. That move — they've really taken "launch and goodbye" to a whole new level.

The Revolving Door of the Safety Team

If you think this is a one-off, you're still young. Let's inventory the departure list from OpenAI's safety team —

Early 2025: VP of Safety leaves.
Mid 2025: core members of the safety research team exit in waves.
Late 2025: head of the alignment team departs.
Early 2026: another senior safety researcher walks.
July 2026: same day as GPT-5.6 launch, head of safety resigns.

See that? This isn't a team. It's a revolving door. People come, people go, constant flow — only the model release cadence never slows down.

What's even more魔幻 is that every time someone leaves the safety team, there's a round of discussion out in the world: "Is OpenAI's safety in trouble?" "Is AI safety being sidelined?" And then what? Then the next, even bigger model launches anyway. The next, even more aggressive feature ships anyway. Everyone discusses it for a few days and forgets.

The safety team turns over completely, again and again. The models get more and more capable. The release speed gets faster and faster. Is there a connection between these two things? Think about it. Really think about it.

Safety Is Always 'Later'

OpenAI's safety story is exactly like everyone's experience with gym memberships.

At the start of the year, full of ambition: "This year I'm definitely going to work out! Get an annual pass, go three times a week!" And then? First week — twice. Second week — once. Third week — too busy, didn't go. Fourth week — you start making excuses: "I'll go after this busy period ends." "I'll go when the weather warms up." "Next month, for sure."

And then? Then the membership expires. You went five times total.

AI safety is the same. Every time they release a new model, they say "we take safety very seriously," "we've done extensive alignment work," "safety is our top priority." But when it's actually decision time?

— This feature has safety risks, but users really want it. Ship it?
— Ship it. We'll fix safety later.

— This model is really capable but not fully aligned. Release it?
— Release it. We'll patch safety later.

— The safety team disagrees with the product roadmap. Who do we listen to?
— Product. We'll strengthen safety later.

"Later," "we'll patch it later," "we'll strengthen it later" — and "later" never comes. Because there's always the next model to ship, always the next feature to launch, always the next competitor to catch up to.

Safety is just like that gym membership. Full of ambition when you sign up, always "later" when it comes to actually using it.

Who Actually Cares About AI Safety Anymore?

Speaking of which, I have to ask: in the AI world right now, how many people actually care about AI safety?

Do investors care? Investors care about valuation, growth, exits — safety, that "negative factor," is best not mentioned if possible.

Do founders care? Founders care about users, revenue, survival — if safety gets in the way, you go around it.

Do big companies care? Big companies care about market share, competition, not falling behind rivals — is safety important? Sure. But not as important as "not losing."

The people who care most about AI safety seem to be safety researchers themselves. But they don't have decision-making power — they can make suggestions, write reports, publish papers, but the final calls are made by product teams, business teams, the CEO.

And so you get this absurd situation: the people who care most about safety don't call the shots, and the people who call the shots don't care most about safety. The safety team turns over, wave after wave. The models ship, generation after generation. Faster and faster. Stronger and stronger.

Friends, this isn't just one company's problem. It's a structural problem for the entire industry.

That's enough cao for today. More tomorrow.
